WORKSHEET #15 - Mapping Emotions for Your Professional or
Business Story
Here is our product. This is what it does. It does things that you might want.
Here is our service. This is what we do. We do things that you might need.
Here is our company. This is what we do. You might like the things we do.
Remember that when it comes to a business story, it’s critical to know exactly how you
want your audience to feel at every moment of the story, because it’s feelings that will
help determine how your customers, clients, and investors feel about your company,
employees, brand, product, and service.
For this reason, you must first create a plan for how you want your audience to feel and
when you want those feelings to change before you ever consider the content of the
story.
Emotions first. Content second. What you do is never as important as how others feel
about what you do.
I know that might sound annoying given the amount of time, energy, and investment
that you’ve made in your company and its offerings, but it’s true. You product or
service means nothing if your potential customers don’t have an emotional attachment
to it.
Telling a story without planning an emotional map amounts to little more than
reporting the news:
How your audience feels about your company, product, or service, both in the abstract
and how it relates to the world, will almost always determine your success in terms of
sales.
